data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider American Express (AXP) After Its Strong Multi‑Year Rally
data.description:
• Wondering if American Express at around US$316 per share is still offering value or if the easy gains are behind it? This breakdown is designed to help you weigh that up with a clear focus on price versus fundamentals.
  • The stock has returned 4.9% over the last 7 days and 3.6% over the last 30 days, while the year-to-date return is a 15.1% decline, compared with a 21.8% return over the last year and 105.8% and 128.0% over the last 3 and 5 years respectively.
  • Recent attention on American Express has centered on its resilience as a major payments and credit brand, including continued scrutiny of how consumer spending trends and card member activity feed into its long term prospects. This context has kept investors focused on whether the current share price fully reflects the company’s established competitive position.
  • Simply Wall St’s valuation checks give American Express a value score of 2 out of 6. The next step is to look at how different methods such as discounted cash flow and multiples frame that number, and then consider an even more rounded way to think about valuation at the end of this article.

American Express scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Approach 1: American Express Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company is expected to earn above the return that shareholders require, then capitalizes those “excess” profits into an estimated per share value.

For American Express, the model starts with a Book Value of US$48.80 per share and a Stable EPS estimate of US$20.98 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 11 analysts. The Average Return on Equity used in the model is 36.15%, while the Cost of Equity is US$4.79 per share. This leaves an Excess Return of US$16.18 per share.

The Stable Book Value is set at US$58.03 per share, sourced from weighted future Book Value estimates from 8 analysts. Combining these inputs, Simply Wall St’s Excess Returns framework arrives at an intrinsic value of about US$391.73 per share.

Compared with the current share price of around US$316, this implies the stock is 19.2% undervalued according to this model.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests American Express is undervalued by 19.2%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 64 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Approach 2: American Express Price vs Earnings

P/E is a common way to value profitable companies because it links what you pay for each share directly to the earnings that each share generates. Investors usually accept a higher or lower P/E depending on what they expect for future earnings growth and how much risk they see in those earnings.

American Express currently trades on a P/E of 20.27x. That sits above the Consumer Finance industry average P/E of 8.48x, but slightly below the peer group average of 21.72x. On its own, that mix of numbers is hard to interpret because peers and industry may have very different growth profiles, margins and risk levels.

To refine this, Simply Wall St calculates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” for American Express of 18.78x. This is intended to be the P/E you might expect given factors such as earnings growth, profitability, industry, market cap and specific risks. Because it is tailored to the company rather than broad groups, it can be more informative than simple peer or industry comparisons. Set against the current P/E of 20.27x, American Express trades above this Fair Ratio. This points to the shares looking overvalued on this measure.

data.title: Sankyo Tateyama (TSE:5932) Returns To Q3 Profit Challenging Persistent Loss Narratives
data.description:
Sankyo Tateyama (TSE:5932) has posted its Q3 2026 numbers with revenue of ¥84,213 million and basic EPS of ¥2.33, alongside net income of ¥73 million that contrasts with prior quarterly losses in the trailing figures. Over recent quarters, revenue has ranged between ¥84,043 million and ¥93,713 million while basic EPS has swung from a loss of ¥61.37 to a small profit of ¥0.35 before Q3, giving a picture of a business where the top line has been relatively steady but earnings have been more volatile. For investors, the latest results indicate that margins remain under pressure even though the income statement has moved back into positive territory.

See our full analysis for Sankyo TateyamaInc.

With the headline results on the table, the next step is to see how these figures line up with the widely held narratives about Sankyo Tateyama’s profitability, risks, and potential rewards.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Q3 profit contrasts with ¥3.9b trailing loss

  • Q3 2026 shows net income of ¥73 million and basic EPS of ¥2.33, while the trailing twelve months still show a loss of ¥3.95b and basic EPS of ¥126.11 in the red.
  • What stands out is how this single profitable quarter compares with the bearish view implied by a 59.1% annualized loss growth over five years. Trailing revenue of ¥355.10b and ongoing losses suggest that one positive quarter has not yet overturned the longer loss trend.
    • Critics highlight that trailing net losses widened from ¥863 million to ¥3.95b over the periods shown, which lines up with concerns about deteriorating profitability.
    • At the same time, Q3’s return to a ¥73 million profit shows that results can swing between profit and loss even while the longer term record remains negative.

Low 0.1x P/S sits beside weak cash coverage

  • The stock trades at a P/S of 0.1x versus 0.6x for the Japan Building industry and 1.6x for peers, while debt is described as not well covered by operating cash flow on trailing twelve month figures.
  • Bearish arguments that the discount is tied to balance sheet strain find support in the combination of weak cash coverage of debt and ongoing losses. At the same time, the very low P/S multiple also leaves room for investors to question whether all the bad news is already reflected in the sales based valuation.
    • Bears point to the unprofitable trailing twelve months and poor dividend coverage by earnings and free cash flow as signs that leverage is harder to support.
    • Investors who focus on the 0.1x P/S may see the large gap versus the 0.6x industry average as either a value signal or a pricing of these cash flow and leverage pressures.

Dividend and DCF both flag pressure on valuation

  • The trailing dividend yield of 3.59% is not covered by earnings or free cash flow, and the current share price of ¥696 sits above the DCF fair value of ¥294.67.
  • What challenges a bullish income oriented angle is that investors are being paid a 3.59% yield while the business remains loss making and the DCF fair value is less than half of the current price. This supports a more bearish stance on how sustainable both the payout and the valuation are.
    • Skeptics point out that trailing net income of negative ¥3.95b and weak operating cash flow coverage of debt do not back a comfortably funded dividend.
    • The gap between the ¥696 share price and the DCF fair value of ¥294.67 strengthens the case that, despite the low P/S, the market price does not line up with the provided cash flow estimate.

data.title: Is It Time To Reassess Heineken (ENXTAM:HEIA) After Recent Share Price Weakness
data.description:

Approach 1: Heineken Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes the cash Heineken is expected to generate in the future and discounts those amounts back into today’s euros to estimate what the business could be worth right now.

Heineken’s latest twelve month free cash flow stands at about €2.57b. Using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model, analyst estimates are used for the next few years, and Simply Wall St then extrapolates further out. For example, free cash flow is projected at €3.23b in 2028, and the model continues with ten year projections, all translated back into today’s value using a discount rate.

On this basis, the DCF model suggests an intrinsic value of about €172.69 per share. Compared with the current share price of around €69.08, the model implies roughly a 60.0% discount, which indicates that Heineken shares are trading well below this estimate of fair value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Heineken.

Approach 2: Heineken Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like Heineken, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for what investors are currently willing to pay for each euro of earnings. It links directly to the business’s ability to generate profit today, which many investors use as a starting point when thinking about value.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while lower growth expectations or higher risk often go with a lower P/E.

Heineken currently trades on a P/E of about 20.37x. That is close to the peer average of 20.85x and above the Beverage industry average of 16.89x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Heineken is 21.82x, which is its proprietary view of what a justified P/E might be, given factors such as earnings growth, industry, profit margin, market cap and risk profile.

The Fair Ratio aims to be more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the wider industry because it blends these company specific characteristics into a single reference point. Against this 21.82x Fair Ratio, Heineken’s current 20.37x P/E sits below that level, which points to the shares trading at a discount to this Fair Ratio based view of value.

data.title: Cigna Revenue Grows As Shrinking Customer Base Raises Competitive Questions
data.description:
• Cigna Group, NYSE:CI, is reporting steady revenue growth alongside a significant decline in its customer base over the past two years.
  • This combination suggests product and competitive pressures that are starting to reshape how the company earns its revenue.
  • The shift in customer numbers is becoming a key issue for investors tracking Cigna's long term position in health benefits and related services.

Cigna Group, NYSE:CI, recently closed at $277.56, with the share price up 3.8% over the past week and 2.9% over the past month. Over longer periods, returns of 12.6% over three years and 21.8% over five years sit alongside a 1 year decline of 11.4% and a small year to date decline of 0.6%. This mix suggests the market is weighing Cigna's track record against fresh concerns around customer losses.

The gap between revenue growth and a shrinking customer base points to underlying shifts that readers will want to understand. These include how Cigna is pricing products, how it is competing for members, and how it is managing its core health benefits franchise. The following sections break down what this customer trend could mean for NYSE:CI, how it might affect business mix over time, and what questions investors may want to keep on their radar.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Cigna Group by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Cigna Group.

📰 Beyond the headline: 1 risk and 6 things going right for Cigna Group that every investor should see.

The tension for Cigna is that 11.4% annualized revenue growth over five years sits alongside an average 12.1% year on year decline in its customer base over the last two. That suggests the business model is leaning more on higher pricing, product mix and larger accounts than on broad membership growth. For a health benefits player that competes with UnitedHealth, Elevance and CVS Health, shrinking member counts can point to product gaps, tougher renewal cycles or loss of price competitiveness. At the same time, Cigna’s large scale and supplier negotiating leverage still matter in a volume driven industry, especially in areas like pharmacy benefits and specialty drugs. For you as an investor, the key question is whether the customer contraction reflects a deliberate exit from lower margin segments or whether it signals pressure that could eventually weigh on revenue and earnings if it continues.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Cigna Group Narrative

  • The focus on higher margin segments such as specialty pharmacy and health services lines up with the idea that management is prioritizing quality of revenue over sheer membership counts.
  • The reported 12.1% annual decline in customers over two years challenges the narrative assumption that demographic trends will naturally support member growth across Cigna Healthcare.
  • The narrative highlights regulatory and affordability risks around the Evernorth PBM model, but it does not fully address what sustained membership losses could mean for pricing power in future contract negotiations.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Cigna Group to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ A 12.1% average annual decline in the customer base over two years may indicate product or pricing weaknesses that could make it harder to compete with large peers on new group contracts.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged 1 important risk, and Cigna’s heavy reliance on Evernorth’s PBM model could compound pressure if regulators or clients push back on drug pricing structures.
  • 🎁 Revenue growth of 11.4% per year over five years shows Cigna has been able to grow the top line despite membership pressure, suggesting some resilience in its core franchises.
  • 🎁 Scale in pharmacy benefits and specialty services may keep Cigna relevant for large employers that want integrated health solutions, even if smaller or lower margin segments shrink.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on whether Cigna can stabilize or improve member trends while maintaining its revenue trajectory. Watch disclosures on where membership is being lost, such as specific employer groups or government related programs, and whether pricing actions lead to further churn. Track how management talks about Evernorth client wins or losses, since PBM contracts can influence both revenue and customer stickiness. Competitive moves from UnitedHealth, Elevance and CVS Health in specialty pharmacy and integrated care will also help you gauge whether Cigna’s product suite is keeping pace or falling behind.

data.title: Does LPS Brasil - Consultoria de Imóveis (BVMF:LPSB3) Deserve A Spot On Your Watchlist?
data.description:
It's common for many investors, especially those who are inexperienced, to buy shares in companies with a good story even if these companies are loss-making. But the reality is that when a company loses money each year, for long enough, its investors will usually take their share of those losses. Loss-making companies are always racing against time to reach financial sustainability, so investors in these companies may be taking on more risk than they should.

Despite being in the age of tech-stock blue-sky investing, many investors still adopt a more traditional strategy; buying shares in profitable companies like LPS Brasil - Consultoria de Imóveis (BVMF:LPSB3). Now this is not to say that the company presents the best investment opportunity around, but profitability is a key component to success in business.

LPS Brasil - Consultoria de Imóveis' Earnings Per Share Are Growing

If a company can keep growing earnings per share (EPS) long enough, its share price should eventually follow. So it makes sense that experienced investors pay close attention to company EPS when undertaking investment research. Recognition must be given to the that LPS Brasil - Consultoria de Imóveis has grown EPS by 55% per year, over the last three years. Growth that fast may well be fleeting, but it should be more than enough to pique the interest of the wary stock pickers.

One way to double-check a company's growth is to look at how its revenue, and earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) margins are changing. LPS Brasil - Consultoria de Imóveis maintained stable EBIT margins over the last year, all while growing revenue 5.6% to R$203m. That's encouraging news for the company!

Theory would suggest that it's an encouraging sign to see high insider ownership of a company, since it ties company performance directly to the financial success of its management. So as you can imagine, the fact that LPS Brasil - Consultoria de Imóveis insiders own a significant number of shares certainly is appealing. Owning 50% of the company, insiders have plenty riding on the performance of the the share price. This should be a welcoming sign for investors because it suggests that the people making the decisions are also impacted by their choices. In terms of absolute value, insiders have R$120m invested in the business, at the current share price. That's nothing to sneeze at!

Is LPS Brasil - Consultoria de Imóveis Worth Keeping An Eye On?

LPS Brasil - Consultoria de Imóveis' earnings per share growth have been climbing higher at an appreciable rate. That EPS growth certainly is attention grabbing, and the large insider ownership only serves to further stoke our interest. At times fast EPS growth is a sign the business has reached an inflection point, so there's a potential opportunity to be had here. Based on the sum of its parts, we definitely think its worth watching LPS Brasil - Consultoria de Imóveis very closely. Approach 1: ING Groep Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how efficiently ING Groep is using shareholder capital, comparing the returns it generates to the cost of that capital. Instead of focusing on cash flows, it focuses on earnings and book value to estimate what each share could be worth if those economics stay broadly similar over time.

For ING Groep, the current Book Value is €18.63 per share and the Stable Book Value estimate is €18.68 per share, based on weighted future Book Value estimates from 11 analysts. Stable EPS is €2.63 per share, sourced from weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 15 analysts. Against a Cost of Equity of €1.17 per share, this implies an Excess Return of €1.45 per share and an Average Return on Equity of 14.06%.

Feeding these inputs into the Excess Returns model produces an estimated intrinsic value of about €52.47 per share. Compared with the current share price of €24.20, this suggests the stock is 53.9% undervalued according to this approach.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: ING Groep Price vs Earnings

For a profitable bank like ING Groep, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to link what you pay today with the earnings the business is generating. It helps you see how many euros investors are currently willing to pay for each euro of profit.

What counts as a "normal" or "fair" P/E depends on how fast earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings are. Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk usually support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk often go with a lower P/E.

ING Groep currently trades on a P/E of 8.35x. That sits below the Banks industry average P/E of 11.14x and below the broader peer average of 13.81x. data.title: Is Visteon (VC) Still Attractive After A 33% One Year Share Price Gain
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Visteon at around US$97 per share is still offering value, it helps to look past the headline price and focus on what the current valuation is really telling you.
  • The stock has returned 4.7% over the last 7 days, 5.7% over 30 days, 0.1% year to date and 32.7% over the past year. The 3 year and 5 year returns of 33.7% and 17.9% highlight that the longer term experience has been more mixed.
  • Recent coverage of Visteon has focused on its position in the auto components space and how investors are reassessing companies tied to vehicle production and technology. This context helps explain why sentiment around the stock can shift as investors weigh its role in the sector against broader market conditions.
  • Visteon currently has a valuation score of 4 out of 6. Next up is a closer look at traditional valuation methods like DCFs and multiples, along with a more rounded way to think about value that will be covered at the end of the article.

Visteon delivered 32.7% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Auto Components industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Visteon Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of the cash Visteon could generate in the future and discounts those amounts back to today, to arrive at an estimated value per share in today’s dollars.

For Visteon, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flows in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is reported at $281.56 million. Analysts provide explicit free cash flow estimates out to 2030, for example $240.40 million in 2030, and Simply Wall St extrapolates further projections from there using the same framework.

After discounting these projected cash flows, the DCF model indicates an estimated intrinsic value of about $154.37 per share, compared with the current share price of around $97. This implies a 37.2% discount to the model’s estimate. This suggests the shares may be trading below this DCF based value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Visteon is undervalued by 37.2%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 64 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Visteon.

Approach 2: Visteon Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a handy shorthand for how much you are paying for each dollar of current earnings, which makes it a useful cross check against the DCF result you saw earlier.

What counts as a "normal" P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually points to a lower, more cautious multiple.

Visteon currently trades on a P/E of 12.95x. That sits below both the Auto Components industry average of 16.32x and the peer group average of 16.21x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary "Fair Ratio" of 12.89x for Visteon. This Fair Ratio reflects factors such as earnings growth, industry, profit margins, market cap and company specific risks, rather than just lining it up against broad industry or peer averages.

Because the Fair Ratio is tailored to Visteon’s own profile, it can be a more precise guide than simple peer comparisons. With the actual P/E of 12.95x sitting very close to the Fair Ratio of 12.89x, the shares look priced at about the level this framework would suggest.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Visteon Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. Narratives on Simply Wall St let you turn your view of Visteon into a clear story that links what you think will happen to its revenue, earnings and margins, to a financial forecast, and then to a fair value that you can compare with the current price on the Community page. That fair value updates automatically when new news or earnings arrive. For example, one investor might build a Narrative that lines up with the higher US$130 analyst fair value and sees Visteon’s AI cockpit and ADAS platforms as a strong long term opportunity. Another might lean closer to the lower US$102 view and focus more on tariff risks, OEM insourcing and competition. This gives you a simple way to see where your own view sits between those ends of the range.
